goal: Cook food using intense radiant heat from above to quickly brown the surface and cook through, creating a charred, caramelized exterior.
when_to_use: When a quick, high-heat cooking method is needed for tender cuts of meat, fish fillets, or vegetables that benefit from surface browning and charring.
inputs: protein or vegetable (tender cuts, thin pieces)
equipment: broiler pan or sheet pan with rack
steps: Position oven rack 2–4 inches (5–10 cm) from the broiler element.
Preheat broiler on high for 10–15 minutes until the element is glowing red.
Pat food dry and brush lightly with oil.
Season with salt and pepper or apply marinade.
Place food on a broiler pan or rack set over a sheet pan to allow fat to drain.
Position food directly under the broiler element.
Cook for 2–6 minutes per side depending on thickness and desired doneness.
Watch closely; broiler temperatures can cause burning within seconds.
Flip once using tongs and broil second side.
Remove when surface is browned and internal temperature reaches desired doneness.
Let rest briefly before serving.
critical_control_points: Preheat the broiler thoroughly; a cold broiler will not sear properly.
Position rack distance correctly; too close burns, too far causes steaming.
Watch constantly; broilers cook extremely fast and food can burn in seconds.
Use a broiler pan with a rack to allow fat to drain and prevent smoking.
Flip only once to ensure even cooking and browning on both sides.

failure_modes.case: Rack too close to heat source
failure_modes.result: Surface burns before interior cooks; acrid, bitter flavor.
Slow cooking; no browning; food steams.
Flames char food unevenly; burnt spots; acrid smoke.
Food burns beyond edibility.

signals.visual: Surface bubbles and browns rapidly; edges may char; juices rise to surface. Broiler element glows red.

output.texture: crisp, charred exterior; tender, moist interior
output.appearance: browned to charred with grill-like marks

goal: Cut vegetables into very fine, uniform cubes (1/8 inch / 3 mm) for use in sauces, garnishes, or as a base for fine preparations.
when_to_use: When an extremely fine, uniform dice is needed for sauces, confit byaldi, or refined garnishes where visual precision matters.
inputs: firm vegetable (carrot, onion, celery, bell pepper)
steps: Wash, peel, and square off the vegetable to create a stable, rectangular block.
Cut the vegetable into 1/8 inch (3 mm) planks by slicing lengthwise.
Stack the planks and cut lengthwise into 1/8 inch (3 mm) matchsticks (julienne).
Gather the matchsticks and cut crosswise into 1/8 inch (3 mm) cubes.
All cubes should be uniform in size and shape.
Use a bench scraper to collect and transfer the brunoise.
Optional: rinse in cold water and pat dry for raw applications.
critical_control_points: All cuts must be uniform for even cooking and professional appearance.
Start with a perfectly squared vegetable; rounding prevents consistent cuts.
Knife must be extremely sharp to produce clean edges without crushing.
Work methodically; rushing leads to uneven sizes.
For brunoise, precision matters more than speed.

failure_modes.result: Uneven cooking; some pieces burn while others remain raw; unprofessional appearance.
Crushed, ragged edges; uneven cubes; bruising of delicate vegetables.
Wobbly cutting surface; inconsistent cuts; safety risk.

signals.visual: Perfectly uniform 1/8 inch cubes; clean, sharp edges; no crushing or bruising.
signals.touch: Cubes feel firm and separate easily; no mushiness or ragged edges.

goal: Remove the aromatic outer colored layer of citrus peel without the bitter white pith.
when_to_use: When adding bright, aromatic citrus flavor to baked goods, sauces, marinades, cocktails, or garnishes.
steps: Wash and dry the citrus fruit thoroughly.
Using a microplane: draw the fruit across the rasp in long, even strokes, rotating the fruit to access all areas. Stop when you reach the white pith.
Using a zester: pull the zester across the fruit from stem to blossom end, creating thin strips.
Using a paring knife (for wider strips): cut off a strip of peel, then carefully trim away the white pith from the underside.
Collect zest and use immediately or store in an airtight container.
For zest strips (zest and pith removed), slice into fine julienne if desired.
critical_control_points: Remove only the colored outer layer; the white pith is bitter and unpleasant.
Do not press too hard; excessive pressure digs into the pith.
Zest only the area being worked; avoid going over the same spot repeatedly.
Zest citrus just before use for maximum flavor; dried zest loses potency.
Be careful of fingers; microplanes are extremely sharp.

failure_modes.result: Bitter, unpleasant flavor; can overpower the intended citrus note.
Ragged strips; difficulty removing clean zest; more pith included.
Loss of volatile aromatic oils; diminished flavor.

failure_modes.case: Zesting over open flame or with oil present
failure_modes.result: Oil can ignite; safety hazard.

signals.visual: Thin, even strips of bright-colored peel with no white attached.
signals.touch: Zest feels aromatic and slightly oily; no bitter residue on fingers.

output.flavor: intensely aromatic, bright citrus flavor without bitterness

goal: Remove the individual segments (suprêmes) of citrus fruit, free of membrane, pith, and seeds, for use in salads, desserts, or as a garnish.
when_to_use: When a clean, elegant presentation of citrus segments is desired, free from bitter membranes and pith.
steps: Wash and dry the citrus fruit.
Slice off the top and bottom of the fruit to create flat, stable ends.
Stand the fruit on one cut end and slice downward, following the curve of the fruit, to remove the peel and white pith in strips.
Continue around the fruit until all peel and pith are removed, leaving only the exposed flesh.
Hold the fruit over a bowl to catch juice and segments.
Cut along one side of a membrane, sliding the knife toward the center.
Cut along the other side of the same membrane, releasing the segment.
Repeat for all segments, working around the fruit.
Squeeze the remaining membrane to extract juice for other uses.
critical_control_points: Remove all white pith; any remaining pith adds bitterness.
Follow the natural curve of the fruit when removing peel.
Cut as close to the membrane as possible to maximize segment yield.
Work over a bowl to catch segments and juice; the juice is valuable.
Use a very sharp knife to cut cleanly through membranes.

failure_modes.result: Bitter taste; unappealing appearance.
Torn membranes; crushed segments; ragged appearance.
Smaller segments; loss of yield; broken pieces.
Lost juice; messy workstation.

signals.visual: Clean, intact segments with no membrane or pith attached. Smooth, glossy surface. Uniform size and shape.
signals.touch: Segments feel firm and intact; no ragged edges.

goal: Reduce a liquid (stock, wine, vinegar, or fruit juice) to a syrupy consistency that coats the back of a spoon, concentrating flavor and adding sheen.
when_to_use: When an intensely flavored, glossy sauce is needed for finishing meats, vegetables, or for plating.
inputs: liquid (stock, wine, vinegar, fruit juice, or combination)
equipment: saucepan (wide surface area for faster reduction)
steps: Place liquid in a saucepan; a wide pan increases surface area and speeds reduction.
Bring to a simmer over medium heat.
Reduce heat to maintain a gentle, steady simmer.
Allow liquid to reduce, skimming any impurities that rise to the surface.
Continue reducing until the liquid has reached the desired consistency:
- Nappe: coats the back of a spoon
- Glaze: syrupy, clings to food, leaves a trail when stirred
Season to taste; salt becomes more concentrated as liquid reduces.
Optional: strain through a fine-mesh strainer for a clean finish.
Optional: finish with cold butter off heat for added shine and richness.
critical_control_points: Use a wide pan to maximize evaporation and reduce cooking time.
Maintain a gentle simmer; boiling can cause scorching and flavor degradation.
Watch closely near the end; glaze can burn in seconds once it thickens.
Taste frequently to avoid over-reducing and over-concentrating salt.
Skim regularly for a clean, clear glaze.

failure_modes.result: Glaze becomes too thick, sticky, and bitter; may burn.
Cloudy glaze; flavor becomes harsh; may break if emulsion-based.
Cloudy, unappealing glaze with off-flavors.
Inedibly salty as liquid concentrates.

signals.visual: Volume reduces significantly; liquid becomes syrupy and coats the back of a spoon; bubbles become larger and slower as liquid thickens. When stirred, a trail remains briefly.
signals.sound: Bubbling sound becomes slower and more concentrated as liquid reduces.
signals.touch: Glaze feels thick and sticky; leaves a coating on the spoon.

output.flavor: intensely concentrated, savory or sweet depending on base

goal: Cook food in a moderate amount of hot oil (about halfway up the food) to achieve a crisp, browned exterior.
when_to_use: When cooking foods that are too thick for sautéing but not large enough to require deep frying, such as chicken cutlets, fish fillets, or potato cakes.
inputs: high smoke point oil (canola, vegetable, peanut)
steps: Pour oil into a heavy skillet to a depth of about 1/4 to 1/2 inch (6–12 mm).
Prepare food: pat dry, season, and dredge in flour, egg, and breadcrumbs if desired.
Test oil temperature with a small piece of food; it should bubble vigorously.
Place food in the pan in a single layer, leaving space between pieces.
Cook until golden brown on the first side, about 2–4 minutes depending on thickness.
Flip carefully and cook until second side is golden brown and food is cooked through.
Remove with tongs and drain on a wire rack over paper towels.
Season immediately with salt.
critical_control_points: Maintain consistent oil temperature; too low causes greasy food, too high burns exterior.
Do not overcrowd the pan; food should be in a single layer with space between pieces.
Food must be dry before frying to prevent splattering and ensure crispness.
Flip only once for best crust development.
Drain on a wire rack, not directly on paper towels, to prevent steam from making crust soggy.

failure_modes.result: Food absorbs oil; greasy, heavy texture; pale color.
Exterior burns before interior cooks; bitter flavor.
Temperature drops; food steams instead of fries; soggy crust.
Splattering; poor crust formation; dangerous oil spatter.

signals.visual: Food bubbles vigorously when added; turns golden to deep brown; oil surface remains relatively calm.
signals.sound: Loud, steady sizzling; sizzle subsides as cooking completes.
signals.touch: Crust feels crisp and firm; food releases easily from pan when ready to flip.

output.flavor: rich, savory, with characteristic fried flavor

goal: Shape dough into an even, uniform thickness using a rolling pin to prepare it for shaping, cutting, or baking.
when_to_use: When preparing pie crusts, cookie dough, pasta, pizza bases, or any dough that requires a uniform thickness.
equipment: optional: rolling pin rings or guides
steps: Lightly flour the work surface and rolling pin.
Shape the dough into a flat disc or rectangle (for pasta).
Place dough on the floured surface.
Press the rolling pin gently into the center of the dough and roll outward, applying even pressure.
Rotate the dough 90 degrees after each roll to maintain an even circle or shape.
Lift and turn the dough periodically to ensure it isn't sticking.
Dust with additional flour as needed to prevent sticking, but avoid excess.
Roll to the desired thickness, checking with a ruler or by eye.
For pasta, fold and roll repeatedly to develop gluten and achieve thinness.
If dough becomes too soft, chill for 15–30 minutes before continuing.
critical_control_points: Start with chilled dough; warm dough becomes sticky and difficult to roll.
Apply even pressure; uneven pressure creates uneven thickness.
Rotate dough frequently to maintain shape and prevent sticking.
Dust with flour sparingly; excess flour dries out dough and prevents proper sealing.
For flaky pastries, work quickly to keep butter cold; warm butter melts into dough.

control_variables.heat_level: cold for pastry, ambient for pasta

failure_modes.result: Sticky, difficult to handle; butter melts out (for pastry); dough tears.
Inconsistent thickness; uneven baking; some areas burn while others are underbaked.
Dough sticks; shape becomes irregular; uneven rolling.
Dough becomes dry and tough; layers may not seal (for laminated doughs).

signals.visual: Dough maintains consistent thickness throughout; edges are smooth and even; no visible cracks.
signals.touch: Dough feels supple and even; no thick or thin spots; moves freely on work surface without sticking.

goal: Apply sauce to a plated dish in an intentional, aesthetically pleasing manner that complements the food and enhances the dining experience.
when_to_use: When finishing a plated dish that will be served with a sauce, in restaurants or when presentation matters.
steps: Ensure sauce is at the proper temperature and consistency; warm sauces should be hot, cold sauces chilled.
Choose an application method based on desired effect:
- Under: Pool sauce under the main component.
- Over: Ladle or spoon sauce over the main component.
- Around: Swirl or pool sauce around the main component.
- Drizzle: Use a squeeze bottle or spoon to create lines or dots.
- Swoosh: Use the back of a spoon to create a curved stroke.
- Dot: Place small dots of sauce around the plate.
Apply sauce to the plate, not over the food, if multiple sauces are used to maintain separation.
If using multiple sauces, apply each separately and ensure they do not run into each other unless intended.
Wipe any drips or smudges from the plate rim.
Serve immediately.
critical_control_points: Sauce consistency must be appropriate for the application; too thin runs, too thick doesn't flow.
Sauce temperature must be correct; warm sauce on cold plates cools quickly.
Work quickly to maintain food temperature.
Do not oversauce; sauce should complement, not overwhelm.
Plate rim must be clean; drips appear unprofessional.

failure_modes.result: Runs off food or plate; pools unattractively; fails to cling.
Lumps on plate; difficult to apply evenly; looks heavy.
Overwhelms the main component; unattractive appearance.
Unprofessional appearance; suggests lack of care.
Uneven coverage; unattractive presentation.

signals.visual: Sauce placed with intention; complements the main component without overwhelming; plate rim clean; pattern or placement enhances the dish.
signals.touch: Sauce is at proper serving temperature.

output.flavor: sauce complements and enhances main component

goal: Caramelize the surface of a meringue using a kitchen torch to create a browned, crisp exterior with a soft interior.
when_to_use: When finishing desserts with meringue toppings such as baked Alaska, lemon meringue pie, or pavlova for a dramatic, toasted appearance.
inputs: meringue-topped dessert (lemon pie, baked Alaska, etc.)
kitchen torch with butane or propane
equipment: long lighter (if torch doesn't have piezo ignition)
steps: Ensure the dessert is fully assembled and chilled (for baked Alaska) or at room temperature.
Check the torch: ensure fuel is adequate and connections are secure.
Hold the torch 2–4 inches (5–10 cm) from the meringue surface.
Ignite the torch and adjust flame to a medium intensity.
Move the flame continuously over the meringue surface in a sweeping motion.
Do not hold the flame in one spot; this causes burning.
Watch as the meringue browns; color will develop quickly.
Continue until the entire surface is uniformly golden brown with some darker spots for visual appeal.
Serve immediately while the contrast between crisp exterior and soft interior is at its peak.
critical_control_points: Keep the torch moving constantly to avoid burning.
Maintain proper distance; too close burns, too far does nothing.
Do not torch near flammable materials; remove paper liners, napkins, etc.
Let the torch cool before storing; butane torches get hot.
For baked Alaska, ensure the meringue is thick enough to insulate the ice cream; thin meringue will transfer heat and melt ice cream.

failure_modes.result: Burnt, black spots; bitter flavor; uneven appearance.
Burning; meringue catches fire; melted ice cream underneath.
No browning; meringue warms but doesn't caramelize.
Fire hazard; injury risk.

signals.visual: Meringue surface transforms from white to golden brown; darker spots develop for contrast; no black, burnt areas.
signals.sound: Gentle hissing of torch; subtle crackling as meringue caramelizes.

output.texture: crisp, caramelized exterior; soft, marshmallow-like interior
output.appearance: golden brown, evenly toasted, with dramatic visual appeal
